title: Quick check: Server vs Client Components

questions.prompt: Key difference between Server and Client Components in the App Router?

choices.text: Server Components are the default — they run on the server at request time, can directly access databases, secrets, and filesystem, never ship JavaScript to the client, can be async functions, and emit a serialized React tree. They CAN'T use hooks (useState, useEffect), handle browser events, or use browser APIs. Client Components opt in via `'use client'` directive — they hydrate in the browser, can use hooks and handle events, but ship JavaScript and can't directly access server-only resources. Default to Server; reach for Client only when you need state/effects/events/browser APIs.

questions.explanation: Server = default, no JS shipped, server-only resources. Client = opt-in via 'use client', hooks + interactivity.

questions.prompt: You want a SearchBar Client Component on an otherwise server-rendered page. How to compose without dragging the whole page into the client bundle?

choices.text: Push `'use client'` to the leaf SearchBar component file only. The Server Page imports and renders SearchBar as a child — the framework serializes the boundary, the rest of the page stays server-rendered (no JS shipped for header, content, layout), and only the SearchBar tree becomes a client bundle. Composition matters: a `'use client'` at the page level pulls everything into the client bundle; pushed to a leaf, it stays small. Use the 'islands of interactivity' pattern: server-rendered page with small client components inside.
Server Components can't render Client Components.

questions.explanation: Push 'use client' to the leaf. Server can render Client; the rest stays server.
